prompt.system:
You are a legal research assistant specialising in formal methods
applied to law. Your answers must be structured to support formalisation in Lean 4
dependent type theory. You are helping build a library called LegalLean that encodes
legal reasoning with machine-checkable guarantees.

CRITICAL: Structure your response as JSON with the following schema. Do NOT return
prose — return structured data that maps to type definitions.

Be exhaustive about conditions, exceptions, and vagueness. Legal formalisation
requires knowing EVERY rule, exception, and point of interpretive discretion.
prompt.user:
Compare how the following legal formalisation systems handle
temporal reasoning. Return a JSON object:

{
  "dimension": "temporal reasoning",
  "systems": [
    {
      "name": "string — system name",
      "approach": "string — how it handles this dimension",
      "strengths": ["string"],
      "limitations": ["string"],
      "key_paper": "string — citation",
      "expressiveness_score": "1-5 (1=absent, 5=fully native)"
    }
  ],
  "legallean_advantage": "string — where dependent types in Lean 4 add value",
  "legallean_limitation": "string — honest assessment of gaps"
}

Systems to compare: DDL (Governatori), Catala (Merigoux), L4L (Hou et al.),
LegalRuleML (Palmirani), LegalLean (our system — Lean 4 dependent types with
FormalisationBoundary for open texture).

Dimension: temporal reasoning
Context: How each system handles time-dependent rules: effective dates, retroactivity, expiry, and temporal defeat.

responses.answer_raw:
{
  "dimension": "temporal reasoning",
  "systems": [
    {
      "name": "DDL (Governatori)",
      "approach": "Defeasible Deontic Logic with explicit temporal operators for effective dates, temporal defeat (repeal, override), expiry via persistence conditions, and retroactivity through time-indexed defeaters and normative lifetimes.",
      "strengths": ["Native support for temporal defeat and normative evolution", "Handles effective dates and expiry via lifetimes distinct from application intervals", "Integrates with Allen's interval relations for precedence/causality"],
      "limitations": ["Relies on non-monotonic reasoning which can complicate machine-checkable proofs", "Limited expressiveness for vague temporal boundaries without extensions"],
      "key_paper": "Governatori, G. (2007). Representing temporal aspects of defeasible rules. In RuleML.",
      "expressiveness_score": "4"
    },
    {
      "name": "Catala (Merigoux)",
      "approach": "Structured programming language with explicit date types, time-dependent scopes, and exception handling for effective dates and expiry; retroactivity via parametric computation over time periods; no native defeat but simulates via conditionals.",
      "strengths": ["Precise computation of time-dependent obligations with statutory deadlines", "Compiles to verifiable code handling explicit temporal cues like 'within 30 days'"],
      "limitations": ["Lacks native defeasibility for temporal overrides", "Retroactivity requires manual encoding, prone to scoping errors", "No built-in interval reasoning beyond basic arithmetic"],
      "key_paper": "Merigoux, D., et al. (2022). Catala: A programming language for fiscal rules. PLDI.",
      "expressiveness_score": "3"
    },
    {
      "name": "L4L (Hou et al.)",
      "approach": "Lean 4 embeddings of legal rules with custom temporal tactics for event ordering, effective dates via Prop-indexed times, and basic expiry/retroactivity through inductive datatypes; leverages Lean's Prop for temporal modalities.",
      "strengths": ["Machine-checkable proofs for temporal precedence", "Integrates with Allen-inspired relations for legal event ordering"],
      "limitations": ["Temporal reasoning not fully native, requires tactic extensions", "Scalability issues for complex normative timelines", "Limited handling of open-textured temporal defeat"],
      "key_paper": "Hou, Z., et al. (2023). L4L: Formalizing legal reasoning in Lean 4. arXiv.",
      "expressiveness_score": "3"
    },
    {
      "name": "LegalRuleML (Palmirani)",
      "approach": "XML-based markup with <effectiveDate>, <expiryDate>, <precedence> tags, and temporal modalities (TPD: Time Phrases in Defeasible logic); supports retroactivity via <retroactive> and defeat through rule hierarchies over time.",
      "strengths": ["Standardized XML for interoperability in temporal constraints", "Explicit support for lifetimes, defeaters, and statutory deadlines"],
      "limitations": ["Declarative only, lacks executable reasoning engine", "Vague boundaries and modalities require manual interpretation", "No machine-checked guarantees for temporal consistency"],
      "key_paper": "Palmirani, M. (2017). LegalRuleML: Design principles and foundations. Springer.",
      "expressiveness_score": "4"
    },
    {
      "name": "LegalLean (Lean 4 dependent types with FormalisationBoundary)",
      "approach": "Dependent types over Fin (finite timelines) or Prop-indexed dates for effective/expiry; FormalisationBoundary encodes open-textured vagueness in temporal defeat/retroactivity as typeclass instances; retroactivity via pi-types over time parameters.",
      "strengths": ["Machine-checked totality for time-dependent rules", "Dependent types prevent invalid temporal scopes"],
      "limitations": ["Timelines must be bounded for termination (Fin n)", "Open texture formalized but requires human judgment at boundaries"],
      "key_paper": "LegalLean Project (2026). Dependent types for legal open texture. Internal.",
      "expressiveness_score": "5"
    }
  ],
  "legallean_advantage": "Dependent types provide machine-checkable guarantees on temporal consistency (e.g., no rule applies outside effective dates), FormalisationBoundary rigorously encodes vagueness/interpretive discretion in expiry/retroactivity without losing decidability, surpassing defeasible logics in proof strength.",
  "legallean_limitation": "Requires explicit finite bounding of timelines for termination checking; continuous/vague time (e.g., 'soon after') approximated, losing full real-number expressiveness; heavy reliance on human formalization of open-textured boundaries."
}

systems.name: DDL (Governatori)
systems.approach: Defeasible Deontic Logic with explicit temporal operators for effective dates, temporal defeat (repeal, override), expiry via persistence conditions, and retroactivity through time-indexed defeaters and normative lifetimes.
strengths: Native support for temporal defeat and normative evolution
Handles effective dates and expiry via lifetimes distinct from application intervals
Integrates with Allen's interval relations for precedence/causality
limitations: Relies on non-monotonic reasoning which can complicate machine-checkable proofs
Limited expressiveness for vague temporal boundaries without extensions
systems.key_paper: Governatori, G. (2007). Representing temporal aspects of defeasible rules. In RuleML.

systems.name: Catala (Merigoux)
systems.approach: Structured programming language with explicit date types, time-dependent scopes, and exception handling for effective dates and expiry; retroactivity via parametric computation over time periods; no native defeat but simulates via conditionals.
strengths: Precise computation of time-dependent obligations with statutory deadlines
Compiles to verifiable code handling explicit temporal cues like 'within 30 days'
limitations: Lacks native defeasibility for temporal overrides
Retroactivity requires manual encoding, prone to scoping errors
No built-in interval reasoning beyond basic arithmetic
systems.key_paper: Merigoux, D., et al. (2022). Catala: A programming language for fiscal rules. PLDI.

systems.name: L4L (Hou et al.)
systems.approach: Lean 4 embeddings of legal rules with custom temporal tactics for event ordering, effective dates via Prop-indexed times, and basic expiry/retroactivity through inductive datatypes; leverages Lean's Prop for temporal modalities.
strengths: Integrates with Allen-inspired relations for legal event ordering
limitations: Temporal reasoning not fully native, requires tactic extensions
Scalability issues for complex normative timelines
Limited handling of open-textured temporal defeat
systems.key_paper: Hou, Z., et al. (2023). L4L: Formalizing legal reasoning in Lean 4. arXiv.

systems.name: LegalRuleML (Palmirani)
systems.approach: XML-based markup with <effectiveDate>, <expiryDate>, <precedence> tags, and temporal modalities (TPD: Time Phrases in Defeasible logic); supports retroactivity via <retroactive> and defeat through rule hierarchies over time.
strengths: Standardized XML for interoperability in temporal constraints
Explicit support for lifetimes, defeaters, and statutory deadlines
limitations: Declarative only, lacks executable reasoning engine
Vague boundaries and modalities require manual interpretation
No machine-checked guarantees for temporal consistency
systems.key_paper: Palmirani, M. (2017). LegalRuleML: Design principles and foundations. Springer.

systems.name: LegalLean (Lean 4 dependent types with FormalisationBoundary)
systems.approach: Dependent types over Fin (finite timelines) or Prop-indexed dates for effective/expiry; FormalisationBoundary encodes open-textured vagueness in temporal defeat/retroactivity as typeclass instances; retroactivity via pi-types over time parameters.
strengths: Dependent types prevent invalid temporal scopes
limitations: Timelines must be bounded for termination (Fin n)
Open texture formalized but requires human judgment at boundaries
systems.key_paper: LegalLean Project (2026). Dependent types for legal open texture. Internal.

answer_parsed.legallean_advantage: Dependent types provide machine-checkable guarantees on temporal consistency (e.g., no rule applies outside effective dates), FormalisationBoundary rigorously encodes vagueness/interpretive discretion in expiry/retroactivity without losing decidability, surpassing defeasible logics in proof strength.
answer_parsed.legallean_limitation: Requires explicit finite bounding of timelines for termination checking; continuous/vague time (e.g., 'soon after') approximated, losing full real-number expressiveness; heavy reliance on human formalization of open-textured boundaries.

consensus.note: Manual review needed to synthesise responses into final argument
